vSphere Support Bundle Analyzer

Rapid triage of ESXi vm-support and vCenter vc-support bundles - one HTA, no dependencies, never touches the source host.
Field Manualv1.17.4Windows / mshtaESXi & vCenterGitHub ↗

1. Overview

This tool ingests a VMware ESXi or vCenter (VCSA) support bundle and turns it into a fast, scored investigation surface for two questions: what is this node (configuration, VMs, storage) and what happened on it (logins, actions, persistence, ransomware/intrusion indicators). It runs as a single Windows HTA - no install, no runtime - and reads the bundle read-only. It never runs vm-support/vc-support and never contacts the source host.

Auto-detects the bundle type
ESXi vs vCenter is decided from the tree (etc/vmware/esx.conf vs var/log/vmware/vpxd/) with README / name-prefix fallbacks. The tab set adapts.
Single node or a whole fleet
Triage one bundle, or point at a folder of bundles (a cluster) and get a roster + cross-host correlation and the vCenter→ESXi task chain.

2. How it works

  • Extraction engine: Windows' built-in C:\Windows\System32\tar.exe (bsdtar). No 7-Zip needed for .tgz/.tar.
  • Table-of-contents first: the bundle index is read once (bsdtar -tvf, cached to toc.tsv) so the Key Files board and node summary paint without unpacking gigabytes. Individual files are extracted on demand.
  • Compressed logs: .gz inner logs decompress automatically; .zst (vCenter) needs zstd.exe - the tool probes tools\ and PATH and, if missing, offers to fetch the official build from the facebook/zstd GitHub release (blocked when the online checkbox is unticked - drop zstd.exe into tools\ instead).
  • Outer wrappers: pointing at the outer download .tgz is fine - if the index carries no bundle-root markers but exactly one nested .tar/.tgz, the tool extracts it, magic-checks it, and indexes it in its place (re-runs skip the outer re-index). Multiple nested archives are refused and listed rather than guessed at.
  • Hostile-input guard: a bundle from a compromised host is attacker-influenceable input, so member names are validated at index time - path traversal, absolute/drive paths, control characters, and names that collide on one Windows file are rejected and reported. Rejections are themselves a tamper signal.
  • Deep vSAN paths: vSAN datastore paths contain : (illegal on NTFS); the tool handles bsdtar's :→_ rewrite and reads deep files via stdout to stay under the 260-char path limit.
  • Output: everything lands under _Processed\<host>\vSphere\ next to the app - TOC cache, extracted files, runinfo.json, inventory.json, CSVs and reports.
Requirements: Windows 10/11 (ships bsdtar), run the .hta from a local path (network paths trigger Windows zone restrictions on the UTF-8 reader). Analysis runs read-only against a copy of the bundle.

Evidence integrity & provenance (v1.15)

  • Caches are bound to their source. Every output folder records the source bundle's identity (path, size, last-write) in source.json; analysing a different capture into the same folder wipes the extraction/decompression caches instead of silently reusing another bundle's files. Two hosts sharing a short name in different domains can no longer feed each other stale evidence - and the log says so when it happens. Prefer the full FQDN as the target hostname to keep per-host outputs unambiguous.
  • Capped reads keep both ends. Oversized logs are read head + tail (the unread middle is disclosed inline and in the analysis summary) - the newest activity, usually the incident window, is never silently dropped.
  • Run provenance. runinfo.json records the tool's own SHA-256, bsdtar version, the source signature, which vpxd files were read, every truncation, and every parser failure - a report can be tied to the exact tool build and the exact coverage it had.
  • Timestamps are UTC. Log timestamps carrying explicit +hh:mm/-hh:mm offsets are normalised before merging, so appliance-local events line up correctly against Z-stamped vpxd events.
  • Exports are spreadsheet-safe. CSV fields that Excel would execute as formulas (leading = + - @) are exported inert; JSON remains the forensic-fidelity format.
  • No evidence left in %TEMP%. Temporary .vmx/.vmdk extraction folders are removed when analysis finishes; a failed cleanup is reported with the path.
  • Network is opt-out in one place. The online checkbox (persisted) gates all egress: the GitHub update check, the live NVD lookup, the zstd download and this Manual's fetch. Analysis itself never needs the network - untick it on an evidence-handling workstation.

3. Getting started

  1. Put the .hta in a working folder (e.g. Documents\Claude\Artifacts\vSphere-SupportBundle-Analyzer\) and double-click it.
  2. Bundle field: point at the nested archive that holds the tree (ESXi <host>.tar / vCenter <host>.tgz), the outer .tgz, or an already-extracted folder. Browse… or Pick folder… help.
  3. Target hostname is required - it names the output folder. It is guessed from the bundle name; correct it if needed, and prefer the full FQDN (e.g. esxi01.corp.example, not esxi01) so hosts with the same short name in different domains keep separate outputs. If the FQDN parsed from the bundle later disagrees with what you typed, the log says so.
  4. Click Analyze bundle. The button turns blue when the field points at a single bundle; Triage fleet folder… turns blue when it points at a folder holding two or more bundles.

Log analysis (Logons / Actions / Timeline / Persistence / IOC) runs automatically after Analyze. Use Show log to see extraction and per-log line counts.

4. The tabs

Every table in the app keeps its header row pinned while you scroll, and columns are drag-resizable (double-click a grip to reset).

Tab colours answer "where is there something to read" (v1.16.0). Once analysis completes, the selected tab stays blue, a light-green tab has content, and a light-orange tab parsed empty - the same convention as the Linux UAC Analyzer, so a fleet of empty tabs is visible without clicking through them. An orange tab is a statement about what the bundle captured, not an assurance nothing happened.

Row limits are disclosed, never silent (v1.15.3). Any table with more matching rows than it draws shows an amber ⚠ Warning: Maximum Rows Exceeded chip - hover it for the numbers and the remedy. For the analysis tabs the cap is render-only: narrow the view with the search box, score/date filters or dropdowns, or use Export view → CSV / Copy to Clipboard - exports always write the full filtered set. Two panels cap at parse time instead (VM events keep the newest 3,000; Maintenance events the newest 400) - their tooltips say so and point at the source log on Key Files, because a parse-time drop cannot be recovered by exporting. If the IOC sweep ever stops at its 8,000-hit cap, its own warning names the exact log and line it stopped at and how many logs were never swept - an IOC list that noisy is matching routine traffic and needs tightening.

Overview

Node configuration summary (including Host time zone, read from the TZ token in commands/date.txt - evidence, not assumption) + key-file coverage, the intrusion / ransomware indicator panel, and the CVE exposure panel. The stat bubbles at the bottom (Logons, Actions, Timeline, IOC hits) are clickable - they jump to the matching tab. Buttons: Re-run log analysis and Generate Report (green).

Specification

Managed ESXi cluster nodes (vCenter, v1.16.0) - one row per host the vCenter manages: FQDN and MoRef from vpxd's [vim.HostSystem:host-N,<fqdn>] references, ESXi version/build from the per-host product records the vSAN health capture carries in cleartext, powered-on VM placement from the newest DRS dump, and whether the host appears in the vSAN baseline (obfuscated names recovered by sha256 cracking). Honesty rules: the health capture anonymises which record belongs to which host, so a mixed-build cluster shows the version distribution rather than guessing a binding - and node IPs are not recoverable per-host from a vc-support bundle (vpxd names hosts by FQDN; the health capture hashes IPs without a usable binding). Resolve the FQDNs against DNS, or open each node's own vm-support bundle - fleet mode joins them.

Deep node configuration parsed from the bundle. ESXi: identity (FQDN, UUID, serial / service tag, model/PSNT, management IP + ESXi Host Client URL, and the iDRAC / BMC IP when in-band IPMI exposes it), networking (VMkernel adapters, physical NICs, gateway, DNS, NTP), port groups & VLANs (standard vSwitch port groups plus distributed port groups with their VLANs, parsed from esxcfg-vswitch -l and net-dvs -l), datastores, vSAN & disk layout (disk groups, physical devices, capacity/usage - for recovery scoping), and security posture (AD join via LSASS, VIB acceptance, syslog target). vCenter: identity, management IP + vSphere Client & VAMI (:5480) URLs, SSO domain & identity sources / AD integration, networking, and managed ESXi hosts. The green Export → HTML button writes a standalone node-spec document. (The same identity fields - serial, management IP, web URLs, iDRAC - also appear on the Overview → Node configuration pane.)

Security

A graded review of patch state and recommended security controls, checked against the bundle's config captures and the parsed log evidence, scored as N of M checkable controls at best practice (a bubble on Overview jumps here; the generated report gets a matching section). Three groups:

  • Patch state - build number vs the tracked CVE fix-builds (summarises the Overview CVE panel; advisory, verify against the VMSA).
  • Hardening controls (ESXi) - VIB acceptance, host encryption mode / execInstalledOnly / Require Secure Boot, TPM & measured boot, MOB, account lockout, password policy, shell/SSH idle timeouts, DCUI access, remote syslog, NTP. Settings checks read advanced-list -d (options changed from default), so "at default" is provable, not assumed. vCenter: SSO identity sources, rsyslog forwarding, NTP.
  • Installed VIBs (software integrity) - parses software-vib-list (Name / Version / Vendor / Acceptance / Install date) and flags the rogue-VIB persistence tells: any CommunitySupported (unsigned) package, VIBs from a vendor that isn't VMware or a known hardware OEM, and VIBs installed after the base image (highlighted if within the case window). A Third-party & non-standard VIBs table lists every non-VMware / non-Certified package with its full metadata, risk-sorted.
  • Log-corroborated controls - what the logs show actually happened: SSH logins and ESXi Shell usage observed, logons from public IPs, failed-login volume, and log-integrity gaps. Config claims and observed behaviour are graded separately, so a "disabled" control that the logs show being used still surfaces.

The green Export → HTML button writes a self-contained security report: an executive summary (posture score with fail/warn/pass counts and the top findings), patch state & CVE exposure, ransomware/intrusion indicators, the full control matrix, the third-party VIB table, and the security-relevant persistence/tampering findings - a standalone deliverable distinct from the full report (Generate Report).

Support bundles are targeted: a control whose source file isn't captured is graded n/a - never assumed compliant. Lockdown mode on ESXi 7+ lives in the ConfigStore, which vm-support does not capture. The checks are version-aware: patch-state verdicts select the fix line by the host's version and update level; capture-driven checks degrade to n/a on versions that lack a capture; and the "at default" claims are the documented 7.x/8.x defaults - on other versions the check still proves the setting is unchanged, but flags the default value for verification.

Network

ARP / neighbor cache (ESXi, v1.17.0): the host's L2 neighbors at capture time (localcli network ip neighbor list). Because it is a live command capture, it persists even when an attacker wipes the text logs - on the reference ransomed host it was one of the few surviving records of who was on the segment. The panel cross-joins it with logon source IPs: a logon source that also appears in ARP shares the management L2 segment (management-adjacent); one absent from ARP reached the host routed/remotely. (incomplete) entries are unanswered probes - a scanning tell.

A point-in-time snapshot from the bundle's socket + process captures (ESXi localcli network ip connection list / ps; vCenter netstat -anp / ps auwwx). Three views: listening services (with the owning process and whether the bind is loopback-only or network-facing - an all-interfaces bind from an unexpected process is the review target; a hide loopback checkbox, on by default, hides the loopback-only listeners so the network-facing ones stand out - network-facing listeners are never hidden, and the header counts what was filtered), external connections (established sessions to non-private public IPs - management plane talking to the internet), and a suspicious-process scan (attacker-tool / reverse-shell patterns: nc/socat, /tmp or /dev/shm execs, bash -i, one-liner interpreters, base64 -d, miners). These feed matching Security-tab checks. On vCenter, non-routine appliance shell commands from Linux auditd also appear in the Actions tab (routine VMware automation filtered). A Web access summary panel (vCenter) aggregates the envoy front-proxy access log - the vSphere Client / API request stream - by client source IP (with request/error counts and external-vs-private scope), and scans request paths for web-attack patterns (traversal, injection, exploit/JNDI). Below it, a Recent web requests table lists the newest individual requests (time, source IP, method, response code, path, and the SOAP operation / user-agent), with a 25 / 50 / 100 row selector, a Simplified (login/logoff) checkbox that hides the noisy API/query traffic and shows only Login/Logout events (collected across the whole captured window, not just the newest rows), a hide loopback checkbox to drop 127.0.0.1/::1 and focus on real remote clients (it filters the whole panel - the per-IP summary table and this table, with a note of how many clients/requests are hidden; the checkbox appears in both places and stays in sync), and clickable paths / open raw log links to the source file. These logs are large, so the newest ~12 MB is read from the tail; external client IPs and any attack-pattern hits raise Security checks. ESXi hosts get the equivalent - a Web / API connections panel built from rhttpproxy.log (the reverse proxy in front of the Host Client / API): client source IPs, protocol, and the accessed service, aggregated and listed newest-first, with the same hide loopback filter (most host-proxy traffic is loopback, so hiding it surfaces the real remote clients).

VMs

Per-VM vmware.log events (ESXi, v1.17.0): each VM's own log lives in its folder and covers the VM's whole life since power-on - so the VM-activity view no longer goes blank when hostd's RAM-disk window is short. A tight, low-noise set is pulled: power on (with the vCenter opID that links to the task chain), clean exit, snapshot create (with the snapshot name - a routine backup vs a suspicious manual one), snapshot delete/consolidate, and ISO mounts (a tooling / data-movement vector). vMotion is deliberately skipped - far too noisy on a DRS cluster. On a ransomed host the vmware.logs are encrypted with everything else, so their absence is itself the finding.

(ESXi) Every registered VM parsed from its .vmx: name, guest OS, vCPU, vRAM, vDisk count/names, datastore/vSAN, and running status. Click a VM to open its activity timeline - power, snapshot (take/delete/consolidate), migration (vMotion), and guest-op events parsed from the VM's vmware.log (plus rotations with incl. rotated logs). Each event shows its opID and initiator: an lro-* opID means the operation came from vCenter, and that opID joins vpxa.log/vpxd.log - the vCenter→host→VM chain. Snapshot delete/consolidate right before an encryption window is the ransomware pre-destruction tell. From the timeline, view .vmx config for the full disk paths (recovery). A Guest IP column appears only when a vCenter in the fleet supplied it (ESXi bundles do not carry guest IPs). A Guest OS dropdown (auto-populated from the inventory) narrows the table to one OS - e.g. every Windows Server VM at a glance - and combines with the free-text filter. Green Export VMs → CSV writes the currently-filtered set (the status line notes when a filter is applied).

Key Files

A curated inventory of forensically high-value files (per bundle type): category, description, presence, size, and Open folder / Open file. Blue left-border = critical artifact; an absent critical artifact is itself a finding. Green Export inventory → CSV.

Users

Every account the bundle gives evidence for - the union of config (etc/passwd / etc/group / per-user SSH authorized_keys, where captured) and observed activity (logons OK/failed, sessions, actions, SSO identity events), each row citing its sources. Key bundle reality: vm-support usually omits etc/passwd - but creating a local ESXi account creates a matching group, so on ESXi the group file is the account tell and a non-stock group is flagged NON-STD-ACCT (non-standard, not necessarily new - the bundle carries no account-creation timestamp, so "first seen" is earliest observed activity, never a creation date). Other flags: UID0 (non-root account with UID 0 - root-clone backdoor), SVC-SHELL (service account with an interactive shell), EXTERNAL (activity from a non-private source IP). vCenter rows are largely log-evidenced (SSO/AD principals are not local OS accounts). Click a row for the per-account evidence summary; Export view → CSV writes the full set.

Why a vCenter shows "duplicate" users - and what the tool does about it. SSO logs the attempted principal verbatim, so one human appears as their real account plus every mistyped variant they (or a guesser) ever submitted, and legitimately again under each identity domain. These are not per-node rows - SSO logons are to the vCenter itself; node-local logons exist only in each node's own bundle (fleet correlation joins accounts across hosts). Failed-only principals within edit-distance 2 of an account that successfully authenticated are tagged NEAR-MISS with the real account named; other never-succeeded strings are tagged FAILED LOGONS ONLY. The user dropdown filters by base identity (the part before @), so selecting one name shows its whole variant cluster - domains and typos together - and a Source IPs column shows where each principal was attempted from.

Authentication events. ESXi: auth.log SSH, hostd SOAP logins, shell.log shell-enable. vCenter: websso SSO logins (user + client IP), appliance SSH, vpxd SAML human logins, and binary wtmp/btmp (v1.17.0) - the appliance's OS login records decoded directly from the fixed-record utmp structs: wtmp real login sessions (independent of auth.log's short rotation window) and btmp failed logins with their source host, both method-tagged OS login (wtmp/btmp). A timestamp sanity gate discards a mis-decoded file rather than fabricating rows. Scored for external source IPs, failures, and privileged accounts. Case-window (from/to) filters this tab.

Login sessions (paired) view. Tick the Login sessions (paired) checkbox in the toolbar to switch from raw login events to one row per API session: login time, logout time, duration, user, source IP, session ID, and the client/user-agent. vCenter: built from vpxd SessionManager login/logout calls paired by session GUID; the user comes from the SAML token on the same operation, and the source IP is time-joined from the envoy access log - only when exactly one candidate IP sits within ±3 s (ambiguous matches are left blank with a tooltip, never guessed). ESXi: built from hostd's logged in / logged out events (the logout line carries the API-invocation count and client agent); pairing uses the opID chain, falling back to the login time the logout line itself quotes. Rows are honest about gaps: a session with no logout is still active at capture, past the window end, or a failed login - and is never hidden by the duration filter; a logout with no login means the login pre-dates the captured window. Three noise filters: hide loopback (on by default - drops sessions sourced from 127.0.0.1/::1, the appliance's own services; sessions with an unresolved IP are kept), hide brief <N s (off by default, threshold editable - drops the 0-second automated API polls), and hide unpaired (off by default - drops the blank-duration rows the brief filter deliberately keeps, i.e. sessions missing a login or logout in the window). The row count reports what each filter hid. An svc tag marks solution/service accounts. Row-click opens the full detail; CSV export and Copy-for-notes follow this view. Note: an API session is any client session - the vSphere Client, managed hosts re-authenticating, solution users - not only interactive humans; vpxd.log typically covers only a few hours, so treat the window accordingly.

Actions

VAMI appliance-management audit (vCenter, v1.17.0): applmgmt-audit.log records per-user authorization decisions on the appliance API. Only write operations (create/set/update/delete/…) are surfaced - the stream is dominated by config reads, which are dropped as noise - and the security-relevant ones are scored: log-forwarding changes (an anti-forensics vector) highest, then backup/restore and firewall/network/SSH/certificate changes.

Privileged operations. ESXi: shell.log command history (attacker-typed commands), the vobd audit-event stream (esx.audit.*: SSH/shell enable, account events, lockdown, non-routine firewall disables - boot-replay and patching churn filtered out; rotations always read since the live file often doesn't exist), and esxupdate software changes (image-profile/VIB add-remove batches, with recurring agent re-stages collapsed into one row). vCenter: meaningful vpxd tasks (VM lifecycle, permission/role changes, sessions) attributed to a user (nearest-preceding human login - labelled user approx), appliance sudo commands, and SSO identity events (sso-events/audit_events.log: user/group creation, membership adds, password resets - with acting user and client IP, retention far beyond websso).

Filtering: beside the text/score filters, two dropdowns narrow the table by actor and by kind - each auto-populated with the distinct values present in this bundle (e.g. actor root + kind shell command isolates one operator's typed commands). The toolbar's clear resets them.

Rotated logs: the incl. rotated logs checkbox (next to Analyze) also reads hostd.0.gz, auth.1.gz, … - weeks of extra logon/action history instead of just the live file, at the cost of decompressing each rotation. vobd rotations are always read. vCenter: the vpxd task log is read as a rotation family in chronological order - the newest 4 files by default, 12 with this checkbox - and anything skipped is disclosed in the analysis summary ("N older rotation(s) not read"), so coverage is never silently partial.

Timeline

Merged logons + actions, time-ordered. The Score / tags column is colour-coded, and the Kind column colours shell command red, sudo orange, vpxd/task blue. A multiselect Score filter (≥5 3-4 1-2 0) narrows to the bands you tick, and a kind dropdown (auto-populated from the bundle) shows a single event kind - e.g. only logons, or only shell commands. Case-window filters this tab.

Persistence & tampering

ESXi checks now include VM autostart (vmAutoStart.xml - a rogue VM set to auto-start survives host reboots), and the Specification's distributed port groups show their human names (from hostd/dvportgroups.xml.gz) alongside the dvportgroup IDs.

v1.16.0 adds an Accounts category (ported from the Linux UAC Analyzer where the bundles carry the source): UID-0 clone accounts (etc/passwd, when captured), non-stock ESXi local accounts via the group-file tell, service accounts with interactive shells, and a PAM auth-stack sweep over etc/pam.d (captured in full by vc-support; a stock appliance scores zero) for pam_exec hooks, pam_permit-as-sufficient and nullok.

v1.17.0 adds an Integrity category. ESXi: per-component VIB signature verification (any Failed is suspect), the host audit-record facility state (off = the tamper-resistant audit trail is disabled), a pending image profile staged for next boot, and the CRX container runtime. vCenter: rpm --verify package-tamper detection - tightly filtered to genuine bin/sbin executables and .so libraries with a content (md5) deviation; the routine noise (config drift, Python bytecode, regenerated kernel-module indexes, firstboot-customized data) is excluded so a stock appliance reads clean, and any hit is a review lead, not a verdict. The systemd sweep now also reads unit bodies and flags an ExecStart that runs a shell/download/decoder.

File-inspection findings (verdict: suspect / review / clean / n-a). ESXi: DCUI welcome banner (ransom-note check), rc.local.d/local.sh, remote-syslog state, VIB acceptance, SSH keys, key-log integrity. vCenter: ld.so.preload, rsyslog forwarding, admin systemd units, cron, log integrity. Bundles are targeted - items the bundle does not capture are marked "not captured", not "clean".

IOC hits

An IOC.txt (one term per line - IPs, users, hashes, filenames) placed next to the app is swept across the key logs on analysis; hits are listed here with file, line, and excerpt. Use the orange Create IOC list button (left of Analyze bundle) to build or edit it in a structured editor: IOC.csv holds Value / Type / Note / Case and is the editable source; on Save it is flattened to IOC.txt - the file this tool (and every wrapper in the family) auto-loads at launch. Hash/IPv4 values are auto-typed, and any existing plain IOC.txt is imported on first edit so nothing is lost.

Matching is type-aware (v1.15). The Type column drives boundary-anchored matching: an ip of 203.0.113.7 no longer hits 203.0.113.70 or 1203.0.113.7, hash values bound on non-hex neighbours, and a domain matches its subdomains but not superstrings (evil.example does not hit notevil.example). Bare IOC.txt terms are type-inferred by shape; filename / path / keyword stay substring matches - that is what those types mean.

5. Ransomware / intrusion indicators (Overview)

ESXi kill-chain checklist derived from the parsed data - each is TRIGGERED, clear, or n/a with evidence:

  • SSH / ESXi Shell used · external source IP on login · failed-login burst
  • Host-encryption change / recovery-key access · mass VM power-off / process kill · firewall disabled
  • Payload staged in /tmp or /scratch · download / decoder / reverse-shell · bulk VMDK/VMX manipulation
  • VIB acceptance downgraded (allows unsigned VIBs)
  • Ransom notes / encrypted files on datastore - scans vmfs/volumes for ransom-note files and known ransomware extensions (low false-positive; legitimate appliance disks are not flagged)

6. CVE exposure (Overview)

Advisory panel mapping the parsed build to high-impact vSphere CVEs. Cross-checks: ESXi SLP running state (OpenSLP/ESXiArgs), AD integration (AD auth-bypass). ESXi checks are version-aware: the fix line is selected by the host's version and update level (build numbers are not monotonic across trains - e.g. 8.0 U3f 24784735 < 8.0 U2e 24789317); a train the data doesn't cover is graded verify, and ESX 9.x hosts are pointed at the advisory rather than guessed. Each CVE links to NVD (which resolves per-CVE); the advisory link goes to the Broadcom VMware security advisory index - search the VMSA id shown beside it.

The CVE facts are DATA, not code: cve.json next to the HTA. The tool ships no CVE ids, VMSA ids or fix builds in its source - it owns only the comparison rules, and a build gate fails if one creeps back. Edit cve.json to correct a build, add a CVE, or retire one, then set "reviewed" to today and "reviewedBy" to your initials. The panel and both reports print that review date, and warn once it is older than staleAfterDays (default 180) or was never stamped. With the live check on, most rows come from the vendor instead and cve.json only fills the gaps.
Live check: ON by default, one request per bundle, toggleable. Broadcom is the CNA for VMware CVEs and files structured version data that NVD republishes free and without an API key - including the fixed build per release line. The tool asks NVD which CVEs affect the detected product + version, and takes the fix builds from the vendor's own data, which beats any number typed into this tool. It also picks up the CISA KEV flag, the CVSS severity, and the real per-advisory Broadcom link (not constructible, but NVD carries it). Known-exploited CVEs sort to the top. Every row states its source, in the panel and in both reports.
Why cve.json still exists (the hybrid). NVD's coverage is uneven: older advisories carry no structured version data at all, some CVEs carry a build for one release line and none for the other, and vCenter often carries update labels ("8.0 U3e") with no build to compare. Those rows are answered from cve.json and labelled [cve.json]. It also holds the two config cross-checks NVD cannot do (SLP running state, AD join). Where both have a build and they disagree, the vendor wins and the row shouts, naming both numbers - that means cve.json is wrong and needs correcting.
Air-gapped, offline, or blocked? Untick live NVD check (it persists). The tool then uses cve.json only and says so: "live check OFF - rows are from cve.json only, absence here is not evidence of absence". A failed check (offline, rate-limited, timeout) degrades the same way and names the reason. A failed or disabled check is never rendered as a clean result. If cve.json is missing, empty or invalid the panel says NOT ASSESSED - it never renders an empty CVE section, which would read as "no exposure". NVD allows 5 requests / 30s unauthenticated; this tool makes one per bundle. For a full no-egress posture, untick the global online checkbox next to Analyze instead - it gates the NVD check and every other outbound connection (update check, zstd, Manual fetch) in one place, and persists.
vCenter CVEs are conservative advisories. vCenter exposes only a build number, and 7.0/8.0 build ranges overlap, so the verdict is: patched only if the build is at/above the highest known fix, review if below the lowest, else verify. Confirm against the VMSA and your exact release.

7. Scoring & tags

Rows are scored 0-9; the row tint and Score badge follow it (red ≥5, amber 3-4). Tags carry colours:

IOC SUSPECT DESTRUCTIVE EXTERNAL FAILED PRIV

DatasetAdds score
LogonIOC +3 · external public IP +2 · failed +1 · root/administrator +1
ActionIOC +3 · attacker/ransomware command pattern +3 · destructive vCenter task +2

8. Fleet mode

Point the Bundle field at a parent folder and click Triage fleet folder…. The tool discovers every bundle recursively (extracted trees + .tgz/.tar), skips outer wrappers that already have an extracted sibling, runs the full pipeline per host, and builds a roster sorted worst-first: host, cluster, type, build, VMs, key-file coverage, logons/actions (flagged), persistence, indicators, CVE, and a composite risk score. Click any host to open its full single-host triage; the orange ◀ Fleet roster button returns you to the roster (no re-scan).

9. Clusters

ESXi hosts are grouped by their vSAN Sub-Cluster UUID (from vsan-cluster-get), which also names all cluster members. A vCenter is tied to a cluster when it manages a member host (from vpxd's host-N,<fqdn> map) or shares task opIDs with one. Nodes with no vSAN membership are labelled standalone. This lets a mixed folder of cluster nodes + standalone hosts + a vCenter self-organise.

10. Correlation & the vCenter→ESXi chain

The Correlation button on the roster opens cross-host analysis (computed from the in-memory snapshots - no re-parse):

  • Clusters - members present vs named-but-absent.
  • Source IPs across hosts - an IP on >1 host suggests lateral movement / shared attacker infra.
  • Accounts on multiple hosts - credential spread.
  • IOC hits across hosts and coordinated indicators (the same tell on N hosts = a mass action).
  • vCenter→ESXi task chain - an opID shared between a vCenter vpxd task and the host vpxa that executed it, reconstructing "which vCenter action ran on which host".
  • Unified cross-host flagged-event timeline, merged and time-ordered.

11. Exports & reports

Export buttons are green; on the analysis tabs (Logons / Actions / Timeline / Persistence / IOC) the Export view → CSV and Copy to Clipboard buttons are purple:

WhereOutput
Key Files / Logons / Actions / Timeline / IOC / VMsCSV of the current view
SpecificationStandalone node-spec HTML
OverviewGenerate Report (self-contained HTML with a clickable navigation pane - fixed contents rail, findings count per section, highlights the section you are reading; it prints as a plain contents list. Every tab in one document: node config, maintenance events, the full key-file board, spec, VMs, VM activity, network, indicators, CVE, security posture, persistence, logons + brute-force + sessions, actions, timeline, IOC hits, evidence/provenance)
Fleet rosterRoster CSV · Fleet report HTML (roster + clusters + correlation + chain)
CorrelationCross-host timeline CSV

All CSV exports are spreadsheet-safe: fields a spreadsheet would execute as formulas are written inert. runinfo.json / inventory.json / logons.json / actions.json remain the untouched forensic-fidelity outputs.

12. Caveats & limitations

Guest IPs are not in ESXi bundles (they are guest-side). The VMs tab shows a Guest IP column only when a vCenter in the same fleet reported them and the VM names match. In single-bundle mode the column is hidden.
vCenter task→user attribution is tiered (v1.15). A task line carrying a session[<guid>] is bound to its authenticated user via the SessionManager pairing - exact, shown as session-matched. Without a session ID the fallback is the nearest-preceding human SAML login, capped at 12 hours and shown as user approx; beyond the cap the actor reports unknown rather than a guess. Treat user approx as a lead, corroborate with the Logons tab.
The vCenter→ESXi chain needs a vCenter and ESXi bundle from the same cluster in the fleet; different environments correctly show no shared opIDs.
CVE verdicts are advisory (see §6). Support bundles are targeted, not disk images - some persistence artefacts (cron, systemd-admin, authorized_keys, passwd) are simply not captured and are shown as "not captured".
Row caps exist and are disclosed in place (see §4): analysis tables draw at most 8,000 rows (render-only - exports write the full set), VM events retain the newest 3,000 and Maintenance the newest 400 at parse time, and a stopped IOC sweep names where it stopped. Wherever a cap bites, the amber ⚠ chip appears - if you do not see one, the table is complete.

13. Suggested DFIR workflow

  1. Scope: open the bundle (or fleet). Read the Overview - indicator panel, CVE panel, key-file coverage. On a fleet, sort the roster by risk.
  2. Know the node: Specification for config, networking, and (for recovery) vSAN/disk layout; VMs for the guest inventory.
  3. Access: Logons - external IPs, failed→success bursts, privileged and off-hours logins.
  4. Actions: what was done - shell commands (ESXi) / attributed vpxd tasks (vCenter). Watch the red shell command and SUSPECT/DESTRUCTIVE rows.
  5. Persistence & tampering: boot scripts, syslog forwarding, ransom-note scan, log-integrity gaps.
  6. IOCs: drop your IOC.txt in and re-analyse to sweep the logs.
  7. Fleet: Correlation for shared IPs/users/IOCs, coordinated indicators, and the vCenter→ESXi chain. Export the cross-host timeline.
  8. Document: Generate Report per host and/or the Fleet report for the engagement.
vSphere Support Bundle Analyzer - Field Manual for v1.17.4. Source & releases: github.com/bpmorris22/vSphere-SupportBundle-Analyzer. Times shown by the tool are UTC. Screenshots use synthetic placeholder data (lab hostnames, TEST-NET IPs) and are illustrative renders of the UI.
